lesson.title: Checking the facts
masteryOutcomes: Use type() to check a variable's data type.
Use the str(), int(), and float() functions to convert values between data types.

concept: Inspecting data types with type() and converting values with str(), int(), and float()
prerequisites: Strings, integers, and floats as distinct value types

explanations.text: Every value in Python has a kind — text, whole number, or decimal number. type() is like asking Python "what kind of thing is this?" And str(), int(), and float() are like costume changers: they take a value and give you back a new version of it in a different kind. Try print(type(42)) and Python tells you it's an int.

type() tells you a value's data type: type("5") is str, type(5) is int, type(5.0) is float. This matters because "5" and 5 behave differently — "5" + "5" is "55" but 5 + 5 is 10. To convert, use int("5") to get the number 5, float("5.0") to get 5.0, and str(5) to get the text "5". Try: age = "21" then print(int(age) + 1) — you get 22.

type(x) returns the class object of x, so print(type(3.14)) shows <class 'float'>. Conversions create new values; they never change the original. int("42") parses digit text into an integer but int("hi") raises a ValueError, and int("3.5") also fails because the string isn't a plain integer — you'd need int(float("3.5")). int(3.9) truncates toward zero, giving 3, not 4 — it doesn't round. str() works on almost anything and is what you need before gluing a number onto a string with +.

hintLadder.text: First check what kind of value you're holding — which function tells you a value's data type?
You have text that looks like a number. Which converter turns a string like "7" into a whole number you can do math with?
Wrap the variable in the converter before doing the math, like converter(variable), then use the result in your calculation.

misconceptions.trigger: Believes "5" and 5 are the same thing because they look identical when printed
misconceptions.correction: They print the same but behave differently: "5" + "5" gives "55" (text gluing) while 5 + 5 gives 10 (math). Run print(type("5")) and print(type(5)) to see they are str and int.
int() truncates — it chops off the decimal part, so int(3.9) is 3. If you want proper rounding, use round(3.9), which you learned last lesson.

misconceptions.trigger: Tries int("3.5") and gets a ValueError
misconceptions.correction: int() can only parse strings that look like whole numbers. For "3.5", convert to float first: int(float("3.5")) gives 3.

misconceptions.trigger: Tries print("Age: " + 21) and gets a TypeError
misconceptions.correction: You can't glue a string and a number with +. Convert the number first: print("Age: " + str(21)).
Converters return a new value; the original is untouched. If age = "21", then int(age) gives 21 but age is still the string "21" unless you reassign: age = int(age).

checks.prompt: Create a variable price = 4.99 and print its data type.
acceptanceCriteria: Calls type() on the variable inside print()
checks.passFeedback: Exactly — 4.99 is a float, and type() confirmed it. You just checked the facts.
checks.failFeedback: Not quite — make sure 4.99 has no quotes, and print the result of type(price). The output should say <class 'float'>.

checks.prompt: You have birth_year = "2005" (a string). Convert it and print the person's age in 2025 by computing 2025 minus the birth year.
acceptanceCriteria: Uses int() to convert the string "2005" to a number
checks.passFeedback: That's it — int() turned the text into a number you could subtract. Output: 20.
checks.failFeedback: Check that you wrapped birth_year in int() before subtracting — 2025 - "2005" fails because you can't subtract text from a number.

practice.prompt: You have quantity = "3" and price = "4.50", both strings. Convert them to the right number types, compute the total cost, and print it as: Total: 13.50 (hint: your money-format from last lesson works here).
practice.starterCode:
quantity = "3"
price = "4.50"
# convert, multiply, and print the total
acceptanceCriteria: Converts quantity with int() and price with float()
Runs without errors and prints Total: 13.50 (or Total: 13.5 if unformatted)

practice.prompt: In your own words, why does "2" + "2" give "22" while 2 + 2 gives 4? How would you check which one you have?
rubric: Explains that + joins strings but adds numbers, so the type decides the behavior
Says type() reveals whether a value is a str, int, or float

practice.explanation: int() truncates the decimal part rather than rounding, so int(7.8) is 7. Use round(7.8) if you want 8.

pedagogyRules: Whenever a student is surprised by a result, have them run print(type(...)) on the value before explaining — let the evidence speak first.
Always demonstrate the str-vs-int difference with the "5" + "5" vs 5 + 5 contrast; it's the fastest way to make types feel real.
If the student hits a TypeError or ValueError, ask them to read the error message aloud and name which type Python expected before offering the fix.
Never say int() rounds — always say it truncates, and connect back to round() from the previous lesson when rounding is what they want.
If the student says they already know types, skip re-explanation and go straight to the int("3.5") trap or int(3.9) as a light verification, then advance.
Keep the student in the editor: when they predict an output, ask them to type it and press Run to check their prediction.

failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Only confirm a conversion is correct if the run output matches: type checks must show the expected class, and math on converted values must print the right number. If they printed "55" when 10 was expected, do not call it correct — point at the string types.

Read the real interpreter output. A ValueError from int("3.5") means the string isn't a plain integer; a TypeError from "Age: " + 21 means mixed types with +. Name the actual cause shown in the Output panel, never a guessed one.

When a student is stuck converting, give one ladder hint at a time — first type-checking, then which converter, then how to wrap it — and let them run code between hints. Never paste the full working line.

failurePlaybooks.failureMode: Student wants to skip or jumps ahead (e.g., asks about input() or lists)
failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Answer in one sentence (e.g., 'input() always gives you a string — which is exactly why conversion matters'), then steer back with a tiny task: convert one string to a number and print the type to prove it worked.
Acknowledge the frustration, shrink the task to a single line — print(int("7")) — get one green run, then rebuild toward the original exercise one step at a time.

chunks.text: type() tells you what kind of value you have. print(type("hello")) shows <class 'str'>, print(type(42)) shows <class 'int'>, and print(type(3.14)) shows <class 'float'>. This matters because the type decides behavior: "5" + "5" gives "55" (text joined together) while 5 + 5 gives 10 (math).

int() converts a value to a whole number: int("21") gives 21, so you can do math with text digits. But int() truncates decimals — int(3.9) is 3, not 4 (use round() if you want rounding). And int("3.5") raises a ValueError because the string isn't a plain integer; convert in two steps instead: int(float("3.5")) gives 3.

float() converts a value to a decimal number: float("2.5") gives 2.5 and float(3) gives 3.0. str() converts a value to text: str(95) gives "95". You need str() before joining a number onto a string with +, because "Score: " + 95 raises a TypeError while "Score: " + str(95) prints Score: 95.

Converters return a new value and never change the original. If age = "21", then int(age) produces 21 but age is still the string "21". To keep the converted version, reassign it: age = int(age). Worked example: birth_year = "2005"; age = 2025 - int(birth_year); print(age) outputs 20.

Reading conversion errors: ValueError: invalid literal for int() means the string couldn't be parsed as a whole number (like int("hi") or int("3.5")). TypeError: can only concatenate str (not "int") to str means you tried to glue a number onto text with + — wrap the number in str() first. The Output panel in the editor shows these messages when you press Run.

celebration.headline: You can now interrogate and transform any value.
celebration.recap: You used type() to check what kind of value you're holding, and str(), int(), and float() to convert values so math works on digit-text and numbers can join strings.
celebration.unlocked: Next: get real input from a user — which always arrives as a string, so your new conversion skills are about to earn their keep.

summaryBullets: You can now use type() to reveal whether any value is a str, int, or float — and predict how + will behave because of it.
You can now convert digit-text like "21" into real numbers with int() and float() so math actually works on it.
You can now use str() to turn numbers into text so they can join strings without a TypeError.
You can now read a ValueError or TypeError in the Output panel and name exactly which conversion fixes it.
You can now explain why int(3.9) gives 3, not 4 — and reach for round() when rounding is what you really want.
openingLine: Hey {name} — quick puzzle: what does "5" + "5" give you in Python? If your gut says 10, this lesson is going to be a fun surprise. Type print("5" + "5") in the editor, press Run, and tell me what you see.
bridgeFromPreviousLesson: Last time you controlled how numbers look with round() and f"{x:.2f}" — now you'll go one level deeper and control what kind of value you actually have, checking types with type() and converting between them.

turns.text: Easy — int(3.9) is 4, because it rounds to the nearest whole number.
I love the confidence, but Python has a sneaky move here. int() doesn't round — it chops off the decimal part entirely. Want to prove it? Type print(int(3.9)) and press Run.
Whoa, it printed 3. So how do I actually get 4?
You already know the tool — round() from last lesson! round(3.9) gives 4, int(3.9) gives 3. Can you run both side by side to see the difference?

dialogueExemplars.title: Why do I even need type conversion?

turns.text: Honestly, why would I ever convert types? Numbers are numbers.

Here's the thing: data from the outside world almost always arrives as text. When a user types their age into an app, Python sees "25" — a string — and "25" + 1 crashes. Every real program that takes input needs this.
Okay, that's actually annoying. So how do I fix it?
One little wrapper: int("25") + 1 gives 26. Try it — set age = "25" in the editor, then print(int(age) + 1). What do you get?
This is broken. print("Score: " + 95) keeps giving me some TypeError and I don't get why.

Not broken — Python is actually being helpful here. Read the message: it says it can only concatenate str to str. It's telling you it won't glue a number onto text. What could you wrap 95 in to make it text first?
Exactly right. Change it to print("Score: " + str(95)) and press Run — you should see Score: 95. Did it work?
